coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled hybrid nanocomposite films of carbon dots and hydrolyzed collagen
coredata.dc:creator: Pinto, Tarciane da S.
Rodrigues, Priscilla N.S.
Marinho, Luiz E.S.
Verly, Rodrigo M.
Bretas Roa, Juan Pedro
de Oliveira, Luiz C.A.
Pereira, Fabiano V.
de Magalhães, Mariana T.Q.
de Mesquita, João P.
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, hydrophilic carbon dots (CD) prepared by cotton cellulose were used in the building of self-assembled films with hydrolyzed collagen (CL) to verify their properties, especially molecular interactions and morphology. The materials were characterized by several techniques, such as AFM, FTIR and UV–Vis Spectroscopy, Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) and Calorimetric Titration. The interaction energy between the two components measured by isothermal calorimetry is higher at low pH due to higher electrostatic interaction. It was observed, in all experimental conditions for the growth of the films, an increase in the amount of deposited material with the number of immersions performed. However, distinct conditions of the protein solutions produced films with different properties. In the CL/CD films, the amount of deposited nanoparticles, with the collagen solution at pH = 3.2, pH = 5 and pH 3.2/0.1 mol L−1 of NaCl, were 2.3 mg/m2, 5.9 mg/m2 and 4.1 mg/m2, respectively. In addition, the increase of the ionic strength of the collagen solution promoted fibrillogenesis of the biomolecule leading to the formation of self-assembled films with a fibrillated morphology. The presented results suggest that the structures of the films can be controlled by manipulating the conditions of the protein solutions. This study may contribute to the development of a future class of devices, such as sensors and photoactive surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrolyzed collagen
Layer-by-layer
Molecular interaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the CL/CD self-assembled films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SPR measurements of the addition of collagen and CD in presence of acetic acid aqueous solution 3% (v/v) at pH = 5.0 (black line), at pH = 3.2 (blue line) and in acetic acid solution 3% (v/v) containing 0.1 M NaCl at pH = 3.2 (red line), on a gold-silicon dioxide sensor. RUeq = Resonance units at equilibrium. (A) Sensorgram of the addition of collagen for calculation of the thickness of the collagen monolayer. (B) Sensorgram of alternate layers of collagen and CD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Isothermal titration calorimetry of 0.21 g/L carbon dots and 2.25 g/L of collagen diluted in acetic acid 3% (v/v) pH 3.2 (A and B), acetic acid 3% pH 5.0 (C and D) and acetic acid 3% pH 3.2 containing NaCl 0.1 M (E and F). The heat flow for each carbon dots injection as a function of time (raw data). (A, C and D) The enthalpy as a function of the carbon dots to collagen mass/mass ratio. The baseline was corrected and the heat of dilution subtracted from each experimental point.

A) UV–Vis spectra of the collagen/CD LBL films prepared with CL solution 3% (v/v) acetic acid solution pH = 3.2. The spectra correspond to 0, 4, 8, 12, 16 and 20 bilayers. B) Absorbance at 400 nm as a function of the number of bilayers. The coefficient of determination (R2) of the linear regression is 0.95.
Effect of the pH change of the collagen solution on the amount of CD deposited.
AFM Images Tapping-mode obtained for CL/CD films of 20 bilayers prepared on glass substrates. A, B) CL solution at pH = 3.2 and C, D) CL solution at pH = 5.0.

Effect of the variation of the ionic strength of the collagen and CD solutions on the amount of CD deposited. The coefficient of correlation of the linear regression of the CL/CD, CL/CD + 0.1 M NaCl and CL + 0.1 M NaCl/CD + 0.1 M NaCl films were 0.95, 0.90 and 0.94, respectively.

AFM Images Tapping-mode obtained for CL/CD films of 20 bilayers prepared on glass substrates with CL solution at pH = 3.2 and CD solution/0.1 M NaCl. B, D and F are 3D views of A, C and E images. The average roughness, RMS, obtained at A was 16 nm.

AFM Images Tapping-mode obtained for CL/CD films of 20 bilayers prepared on glass substrates CL solution at pH = 3.2/0.1 M NaCl and CD solution with 0.1 M NaCl. D and F are 3D views of C and E images, respectively. The average roughness, RMS, obtained at B was ∼15 nm.
Circular dichroism spectroscopy of collagen solutions under different conditions.
Zeta potential of hydrolyzed collagen solutions under different conditions.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled hybrid nanocomposite films of carbon dots and hydrolyzed collagen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química, Universidade Federal dos Vales Jequitinhonha e Mucuri, Rodovia MGT 367 - Km 583, nº 5000, Alto da Jacuba, CEP 39100-000, Diamantina, MG, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade Federal dos Vales Jequitinhonha e Mucuri

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química, Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais, Av. Antônio Carlos, 6627, Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, MG, CEP 31270-901, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Bioquímica e Imunologia, Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais, Av. Antônio Carlos, 6627, Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, MG, CEP 31270-901, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratório Institucional de Pesquisa (LIPq), Centro de Laboratórios Multiusuários (CELAM), Laboratório Multiusuário de Proteômica (LMProt), Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais, Av. Antônio Carlos, 6627, Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, MG, CEP 31270-901, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, hydrophilic carbon dots (CD) prepared by cotton cellulose were used in the building of self-assembled films with hydrolyzed collagen (CL) to verify their properties, especially molecular interactions and morphology. The materials were characterized by several techniques, such as AFM, FTIR and UV–Vis Spectroscopy, Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) and Calorimetric Titration. The interaction energy between the two components measured by isothermal calorimetry is higher at low pH due to higher electrostatic interaction. It was observed, in all experimental conditions for the growth of the films, an increase in the amount of deposited material with the number of immersions performed. However, distinct conditions of the protein solutions produced films with different properties. In the CL/CD films, the amount of deposited nanoparticles, with the collagen solution at pH = 3.2, pH = 5 and pH 3.2/0.1 mol L−1 of NaCl, were 2.3 mg/m2, 5.9 mg/m2 and 4.1 mg/m2, respectively. In addition, the increase of the ionic strength of the collagen solution promoted fibrillogenesis of the biomolecule leading to the formation of self-assembled films with a fibrillated morphology. The presented results suggest that the structures of the films can be controlled by manipulating the conditions of the protein solutions. This study may contribute to the development of a future class of devices, such as sensors and photoactive surfaces.

ce:section.ce:para: Biodegradable and biocompatible electronic devices for monitoring of physiological environment are widely studies to obtain advances in health monitoring and therapeutic technologies [1–3]. In this context, the develop of new hybrid bionanocomposites is a promising research subject that allows the synergism of properties through the self-assembly of biopolymers with different nanostructures [4].

Carbon dots (CD) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials (usually less than 10 nm). It can be obtained from different precursors by using simple procedures. These materials have distinct electronic and optical properties that can be controlled through particle size, structure and surface chemistry [5]. In addition, several in vitro and in vivo studies have shown the low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of these nanostructures [6–8].

Incorporating CD into polymer matrices is a central strategy to expand its biomedical and industrial applications [9]. Bionanocomposites based on carbon dots with superior properties of UV–visible blocking, swelling, low permeability, ultrasound sensitivity, thermal and mechanical properties have been prepared with different polymers such as: chitosan [9,10], polyaniline [11], PEG-chitosan [12], poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) [13], PEGylated oxidized alginate [14], cellulose [15,16], polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) and polyallylamine hydrochloride (PAH) [17]. However, the knowledge about the components interaction of these hybrid nanocomposites, mediated by surface chemistry, is a key factor that can lead to a more rational and optimized development of these surprising materials [18].

Here we have used the layer-by-layer deposition technique (LBL) to investigate the properties of self-assembled films prepared with highly hydrophilic carbon dots from cotton cellulose and hydrolyzed collagen got from industrial leather residues. Hydrolyzed collagen is a mixture of oligopeptides obtained from the animal bones and hides or even from industrial leather residues [19,20]. The LBL technique is based on the bottom-up method and is a powerful approach to construct complicated smart functional nanostructures using non-covalent interactions [21] with wide possibilities of applications [22–25]. Although this technique has been used for decades, it still has an intense and growing scientific interest [26]. The LBL films preparation from incorporating metal-free, non-toxic and photoactivated nanostructures, i.e., carbon dots, can allow the application of multilayered collagen-containing films at different fields, including for example the control of pathogenic microorganisms on surfaces and foods protection [27].

Collagen may be considered the primary resource in biomedical applications and it is one of the most useful biomaterials due to its biocompatibility, biodegradability and weak antigenicity [28,29]. The potential value of collagen, including the collagen film, as a biomaterial has been the focus of the research on three-dimensional scaffolds for biomedical applications, active and functional surfaces and coatings for implants [29–32]. In addition, some authors have shown the biomedical use of collagen for preparing biodegradable packaging material films [20,33]. On the other hand, several bionanocomposites have been prepared between collagen and different nanoparticles, including cellulose nanocrystals [34], silica [35], clay [36] and carbon nanotubes [37]. De Mesquita et al. prepared a multilayered bionanocomposites with the potential to mimic the characteristics of a natural extracellular matrix (ECM) between collagen and cellulose nanowhiskers (CNW) [34]. The film presented a surface roughness with layers of densely packed CNW adsorbed on top of each thin collagen layer.

In this work, we investigate the fundamental properties, i.e. interaction energy and morphology of the self-assembled multilayer films using the layer-by-layer deposition technique of the hydrolyzed collagen with highly hydrophilic carbon dots. We have investigated the effect of pH, ionic strength and the number of dipping cycles, which are the key parameters that control the morphology and the structure of these LBL films [38]. By controlling these parameters, we obtained nanocomposite films with fibrous morphology by fibrillogenesis promoted by the ionic strength of the protein solution. Typically, the natural polymers undergo complicated phenomena of self-aggregation and fibrillogenesis and therefore it is a key factor to know the conditions under which any of these complicated processes are activated for a greater control of the growth process of multilayer films prepared by LBL technique [39].

The carbon dots used in this study were prepared as described in the literature with some modifications [40]. Basically, 5 g de cotton cellulose are dehydrated/carbonized in concentrated H2SO4 for 40 min at 80 °C. The carbon material obtained is further oxidized in HNO3 (2 M) for 12 h, neutralized with Na2CO3 and, finally dialyzed [41,42].

Hydrolyzed collagen was supplied by Vancouros S.A. (Brazil). The collagen solutions were prepared by dissolution, for 48 h, of 10 g of the material in 500 mL of acetic acid (Synth, Brazil) solution 3% (v/v). Undissolved components of collagen were separated by centrifugation and vacuum filtration. Typically, is obtained a solution of 9 g/L with pH = 3.2. This solution was divided into three parts. The pH and ionic strength conditions of two of these fractions were altered. One had the pH changed to 5 with the addition of NaOH and the other, the ionic strength was changed to 0.1 M by addition of NaCl. All reagents were used without further purification.

The self-assembly carbon dots/hydrolyzed collagen multilayers were manually prepared on different substrates (glass and quartz slides). The experimental procedure used here has been described in details elsewhere [34,41,43]. In this process, the slides are sequentially immersed into the solutions until the desired number of bilayers (collagen/CD). In this study the following procedure was carried out: (1) dipping of the substrate for 5 min in the collagen solution; (2) rinsing with deionized water for 2 min in three different beakers; (3) dipping for 5 min in a carbon dots aqueous solution; (4) again rinsing with deionized water. This procedure was used to prepare all multilayer self-assembled films describes in this work. All glass slides were previously cleaned using piranha solution (H2SO4:H2O2, 3:1 vol ratio) and the quartz slides were rinsed with NH4OH/H2O2/H2O (1:1:5 v/v) and HCl/H2O2/H2O (1:1:6 v/v) solutions.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the collagen was recorded with a Varian 680-IR using GladiATRTM attenuated reflectance accessory (PIKE Technologies). FTIR spectrum of the carbon dots was obtained in a spectrometer Shimadzu, model IR Prestige.

The acid-base properties of the collagen and carbon dots samples were determined with potentiometric titration. All the experimental data were measured at 25 °C using a SCHOTT automatic titrator (Titroline 7000). The procedure to the determination of the ionization constants and acid functional groups concentration present on the surface of the materials was previously published [44–46].
The specific viscosity measurement of collagen solution under different conditions was carried out by glass capillary viscometer (size 50) at 25 °C.

Zeta Potential measurements of the diluted solutions of carbon dots and collagen under different conditions of pH and ionic strength were performed using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS equipment (Malvern Instruments, UK).

The determination of the molar absorptivity of collagen and CD were performed in a UV–Vis spectrometer Varian Cary 50. The spectra of collagen solutions were obtained from Circular Dichroism under different conditions of pH and ionic strength (Spectrometer Model J-815, JASCO, Tokyo, Japan). All spectra were recorded at 25 °C by scanning from 250 nm to 180 nm in 1 nm steps.

Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) was performed by using a Microcalorimeter VP-ITC (Malvern) at 298.15 K. Prior to use of the equipment; the solutions were degassed with a Termovac accessory (Malvern) for 5 min to eliminate air bubbles. The experiments were recorded by titration of the CD solution on CL solution, at concentrations of 0.21 g/L and 2.25 g/L, respectively. The first injection of 1 μL was discarded to eliminate material diffusion effects. To carry out the titration it is necessary that titrant and titrate are dissolved in the same solvent. Thus, dry CDs were obtained from evaporation of the aqueous solution in an oven at 70 °C and then dissolved in three different solutions: (i) 3% (v/v) acetic acid at pH = 3.2; (ii) 3% (v/v) acetic acid at pH = 5.0 and (iii) 3% (v/v) acetic acid at pH = 3.2 containing 0.1 M NaCl. Each of the titration experiments comprised 25 successive injections of titrant (CD solution) in the reaction cell containing 1.488 ml of collagen solution. A time interval of 250s was used between successive injections. The first 1 μL injection was discarded to eliminate diffusion effects of the material from the syringe to the calorimetric cell. The experimental data were analyzed using the calorimeter software (Microcal Origin 7.0 for ITC), after subtraction of the respective dilution experiment.

Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) measurements were carried out at a wavelength of 670 nm using a Multi-Parametric Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPRNavi 200, BioNavis®, Finland) as described previously [41]. For all the experiments was used a SiO2 SPR sensor chips (SPR102-SiO2). The concentrations of the collagen and CD solutions used were 2.0 and 1.2gL-1, respectively. All experiments were performed using a buffer (acetic acid 3% v/v in water). The Winspall® (Res-Tec-Resonant Technologies® GmBH) software was used to estimate thickness parameters.
The surface topography of the self-assembly multilayer films of carbon dots/hydrolyzed collagen was examined by an atomic force microscope Asylum Research (MFP-3D-SA) in the tapping mode.

UV–vis spectra of the multilayer films grown on quartz slides were obtained with an equipment Varian, model Cary 50. The spectra have always been obtained after the deposition each two bilayers and the amount of CD deposited was estimated using the same procedure described in our previous work [41,43]. For this estimate, we have disregarded the molar absorptivity of the hydrolyzed collagen.

The carbon dots were prepared by a simple carbonization and oxidation procedure of cotton cellulose. Typically, the nanoparticles exhibit quasi-spherical morphology with a diameter less than 5 nm [44]. The structural, optical and electronic properties of the carbon dots used in the study were published in previous works [41,42]. Given the importance of the surface charges on the growth of the LBL films here, we present only the surface characteristics of these nanostructures. The surface charges of the carbon materials are mainly determined by the acid-base equilibria of the oxygenated functional groups. Typical absorptions of these functional groups can be identified in the FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. S1. The absorption of the v(C-O) around 1100 cm−1 and the intense absorption between 1500 and 1800 cm-1 with shoulders at 1713 and 1770 cm−1 indicates the presence of different functional groups, especially, carbonyl, carboxylic and esters. The presence of carboxyl groups is evidenced by the widening of the v(O-H) band which also indicates the presence of phenolic groups. In agreement with the FTIR spectrum, the pKa values obtained from the potentiometric titration data confirm the presence of carboxylic, lactone and phenolic groups. The pKa 3.16 and 4.55 are traditionally assigned to carboxyl groups and represent over 60% of the total acid functional groups (Table S1). As a result, the zeta potential at pH near 6 is ∼ -40 mV [41]. In the presence of 0.1 M NaCl, the zeta potential decreases to −8.9 mV due to electrostatic screening.

The properties of self-assembled films made with polyelectrolytes are even dependent on surface charges. If, on the one hand, the presence of charges on the polymer chains are the main variable that governs the interaction between the two components during the growth of the self-assembled film, on the other, they determine the conformation of the chains in solution because of the intra and intermolecular repulsions, interfering directly in the morphological characteristics, for example, surface roughness [47]. For biomolecules such as collagen, the change in surface charge density may be determinants for the fibrillogenesis process [30]. Generally, CL has a configuration of three polypeptides, which are attached in a triple helix which is stabilized by hydrogen bonding between the NH group of glycine and the CO group of another polypeptide chain [48,49]. The surface charges on the collagen under determined conditions of pH and ionic strength of the medium can be estimated by the acid-base properties of the functional groups present in the chain terminations and side functional groups of the peptide structure, especially amino, carboxylic and phenolic groups. Table S1 shows the negative logarithms of the acid constants as well as the amount corresponding to each of the acid functional groups obtained from the potentiometric titration curve [34]. The acid functional groups with pKa<5 (2.97 and 4.30) are assigned to the β and γ-carboxylic groups presents mainly in the side chains of the amino acids aspartic and glutamic acids. The pKa 6.47 is attributed to the imidazole groups of histidine. The pKa corresponding to weaker acids, 8.26 and 9.63, indicate the presence of phenolic, amino and ε-amine groups found in several amino acids, e. g. tyrosine, histidine, and lysine [50,51]. The presence of the above mentioned functional groups can be readily identified in the spectrum shown in Fig. S1. The absorptions established at 1655, 1540, and 1240 cm−1 are attributed to typical bands; amides I ν(CO), II δ(NH) and III ν(CN), respectively. In addition, the stretches of N-H and OH around 3400 cm−1 (shoulder at 3288 cm−1) and C-H of sp2 carbon at 3077 cm−1 originated some amino acid with an aromatic ring, for example, tyrosine [52].

Based on potentiometric titration results we can conclude that at pH 3.2 the majority of the functional groups are protonated, with imidazole and amino groups positively charged. As a consequence, the observed zeta potential was +28 mV (Table 1 ). The increase of the pH to 5 deprotonates the carboxylic groups, which counter-balanced the positive charge of the amino and imidazole groups, reducing the zeta potential close to zero. The NaCl addition also decreases the value of zeta potential because of the electrostatic screening effects.

A direct consequence of the decreases of surface charges is the change in the viscosity of the solutions (Fig. S2). When the surface of the chains is charged there is a great intra and intermolecular repulsion generating isolated and extended molecular structures. Under these conditions, the polymer-solvent interaction is strong which results in high viscosity values. The decrease of the surface charges, either by surface deprotonation (pH increase) or electrostatic screening (ionic strength increase) results in a more flexible polymeric chain with more intra and intermolecular interactions, leading to lower viscosity values due to decreased polymer-solvent interaction [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CL/CD self-assembled films
ce:section.ce:para: First, we used SPR to investigate the effect of different conditions of the aqueous solutions on the thickness of the collagen monolayers on SiO2 sensor slides. The sensograms obtained are shown in Fig. 1 A. For all conditions a significant change is observed in the SPR angle after 4 min of injection due to deposition of material on the surface of the SiO2 sensor. After this time, there is a decrease in the variation of the angle which stabilizes at ∼16 min. The layer thickness was calculated using RU values obtained at 3 and 18 min. At pH = 3.2 the thickness of the layer was 0.9 nm, slightly less than to the value obtained with increasing pH to 5.0 (1.0 nm). At pH 3.2 with higher ionic strength (acetic acid 3% v/v solution with 0.1 M NaCl), the collagen layer became even thicker, ranging up to 1.4 nm. According to zeta potentials, at pH close to neutrality or high ionic strength, the surface charge decreases and the polymer chains become more flexible. Consequently, conformational changes allow an increase of intra and intermolecular interactions to form agglomerates with lesser self-organized structures, resulting thicker layers.

SPR was also used to evaluate the capacity of the collagen and CD layers to adsorb each other. A sensorgram containing alternated layers of both collagen and CD was obtained (Fig. 1B). For all conditions studied, the results suggest that the two components of the nanocomposite have higher mutual affinity and sufficient interaction energy to maintain a three-layered structure.

Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) is a highly sensitive tool to analyze interactions in polymer and other materials by their thermodynamic patterns. Beside of the strength interaction, the knowledge of the thermodynamic parameters provided a high detailed understanding of these mechanism of interaction, contributing to a rational design and optimized properties of nanocomposites materials [18]. In addition, the biomolecules adsorption on the nanomaterial are driven by several interfacial forces, which impact on the surface structure and composition of nanocomposite, subsequently manages their reactivity and functionality [54]. Thus, carbon dots and collagen interaction was investigated by ITC under different conditions. Fig. 2 shows the titration of carbon dots on collagen, both diluted in 3% (v/v) acetic acid (pH 3.2) (Fig. 2 A and 2B), 3% (v/v) acetic acid (pH 5.0) (Fig. 2C and 2D) and 3% (v/v) acetic acid (pH 3.2) containing NaCl 0.1 M (Fig. 2 E and 2F). In all cases, significant exothermic enthalpies govern the carbon dots-collagen interactions.

Although similar behavior could be observed, the enthalpy contribution of CD-collagen interaction are much higher in acetic acid solution (3% v/v) pH 3.2 (ΔH o = −74.63 kcal g−1) than the factor enthalpy obtained for carbon dots under the same collagen solution, but containing 0.1 mol L−1 of NaCl (ΔH o = −49.74 kcal/g). The increase of ionic strength, promoted by the presence of NaCl, leads to an electrostatic screening over the collagen [55] and, consequently, decreasing the electrostatic interaction between CD and collagen structures.

On the other hand, the raw data obtained for the assay of CD and collagen in 3% acetic acid solution pH 5, shows a completely different association when compared to the collagen in 3% acetic acid solution pH 3.2. Notably, the CD-collagen interaction in presence of acetic acid pH 3.2 is more pronounced than in acetic acid solution pH 5.0. The calculated enthalpy for the CD-collagen interaction in presence of 3% acetic acid solution pH 3.2 exceed the enthalpy for three to four times for CD-collagen in presence of 3% acetic acid solution, pH 5.0 (ΔH o = −21.26 kcal/g).

These results suggest a lower electrostatic contribution to the enthalpy value, in accordance with zeta potential measurements, which reveals lower surface charge of collagen in pH 3.2 (+28 mV) in comparison with pH 5.0 (- 1.36 mV). We believe that the electrostatic attraction, besides the van der Waals interactions, contributes significantly to the process of formation of the film. The hydrophilic surface for CD exposes a high density of overall anionic charges, which interacts with positively charged collagen. It is important to note that macromolecular interactions are usually complex, especially for biomolecules [56].

In addition, when CD solution aliquots were added to collagen in 3% (v/v) acetic acid solution pH 5.0 it was possible to observe a decrease of the respective heat of reaction (δhi) until values which cannot be distinguished from the heat of dilution of CD at the same acetic acid solution, confirming the lower interaction for CD-collagen under this condition.

The growth of the collagen/CD LBL assembly was monitored by the absorbance measurements at 400 nm. The spectra were obtained after the deposition of each two bilayers on the quartz substrate. The results obtained for CL/CD films prepared with CL solution 3% (v/v) acetic acid solution at pH = 3.2 are shown in Fig. 3 .

Under this experimental condition is observed an increase of the absorption with the number of bilayers deposited. In addition, the absorbance values at 400 nm as a function of the number of bilayers show linear growth, indicating the deposition of the same amount of material in each cycle. This is an interesting result because it allows the control of the amount of deposited material and, consequently the thickness of the film, through a simple control of the number of deposition cycles. Similar results were observed for films prepared with the different conditions of the collagen and carbon dots solution (Fig. S3). However, it is possible to observe a higher absorbance of the films prepared with high pH and ionic strength, suggesting a greater amount of material deposited with the adsorption cycles. Through the molar absorptivity of the CD at 400 nm (2.3 m2g-1), the absorbance values were converted to deposited mass. In Fig. 4 the amounts of deposited material are compared between the collagen solutions at pH = 3.2 and pH = 5.

It is clear that more CD are adsorbed onto the surface of the film with increasing the pH of the collagen solution. We determined the amount of 2.3 ± 0.2 mg/m2 (R2 = 0.95) and 5.9 ± 0.6 mg/m2 (R2 = 0.90), from the slope of the curves for films prepared with collagen solution at pH = 3.2 and pH = 5, respectively. Weak polyelectrolytes such as collagen are strongly affected by changes at the pH of the medium. In addition, the increase in the amount of deposited material and film thickness are directly related to the more flexible conformation of the polymer chain [57,58]. At more acidic pH, the polymer chains are in a more extended conformation, leading to thinner layers with a small amount of deposited material (Fig. 5 ). On the other hand, with pH increase, the polymer chains are less charged and flexible, allowing greater intra and intermolecular interaction forming larger structures. In addition, the polymeric chains more flexible can adopt an appropriate conformation for interaction with the CD nanoparticles. The AFM images shown in Fig. 5B2 show a more rough and irregular surface when compared to the image of the prepared films with collagen solution at pH 3.2 (Fig. 5A2).

Fig. 6 shows the effect of the ionic strength of the collagen and CD solutions on the amount of CD deposited. The deposited material amounts were 2.3 ± 0.2 mg/m2, for CD and CL solutions with lower ionic strength, 4.1 ± 0.4 mg/m2 for LBL films prepared with the increase of the ionic strength of the CD solution and 6.9 ± 0.6 mg/m2 for LBL films prepared with increase of the ionic strength of both solutions. Although the CD does not undergo an alteration of the particle morphology with the change of the ionic strength, the surface charge reduces significantly (see zeta potential), increasing the possibility of interaction and aggregation of the nanoparticles. The adsorption of CD agglomerates justifies the significant increase of material deposited in the film. Similar to that observed with increasing pH, the elevation of the ionic strength of the collagen solution decreases the surface charge, allowing for more flexible conformations and a greater agglomeration of the polymer chains, which also results in an increase of deposited material. The thickness and morphology of self-assembled films prepared with weak polycations are strongly affected by changes at the pH and ionic strength of the solutions, which alter the surface charge and consequently the polymer configurations, interfering directly in the interaction interface. Thus, the thickness and morphology of LBL films prepared with the collagen polycation can be easily controlled by changes of the pH and ionic strength of the solution and also the number of dipping cycles [41,59]. Like chitosan (NH3 +, pKa = 6.5), collagen has several functional groups with weak acidity. Therefore, it was expected to increase the amount of deposited material with the increase of the pH and ionic strength.

In Figs. 7 and 8
the AFM images reveal the presence of fibrils on the surface of the films prepared with the CD and collagen solutions with increased ionic strength. We speculate that the fibers observed mainly in Fig. 8, are derived from the fibrillogenesis process of the collagen chains.

Unlike other biopolymers, both ionic strength and pH may influence the formation of a unique morphology of molecules such as collagen, in a process known as fibrillogenesis, the formation and development of fibrils. The fibrillogenesis of collagens I and II is a self-assembly process governed by hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions which can be formed spontaneously in vitro [30,60].

The secondary structure of collagen is sensitive to environmental conditions and may be disturbed by pH and ionic strength [30,61]. In order to verify this phenomenon, we carry out measurements of circular dichroism spectroscopy (Fig. 9 ). The collagen triple helices were confirmed by the presence of a broad negative ellipticity at 199 nm and a positive at 224 nm for the control sample. When the collagen samples were denatured, the positive peak promptly disappears and the negative peak decreased substantially. These behavior were observed in the spectra of the collagen solutions at pH = 3.2 and pH = 5.0. In fact, AFM images show non-evidence of fibrils on the surface of the self-assembled films prepared under these conditions.

Nevertheless, the spectrum of CL solution at pH = 3.2 with 0.1 M NaCl clearly shows a positive peak at 199 nm and a negative peak at 224 nm with intensities close to those observed in the control sample. When only the CD solution had the ionic strength altered (Fig. 7), the formation of fibrils is less clear and can be caused either by the surface of the nanostructures or by the residues of the NaCl solution at the interface. Aime et al. prepared bio-hybrid networks based on the self-assembly of collagen and silica nanoparticles. They showed that the surface of the sulfonate-modified silica can mediate the fibrillogenesis process [35]. Huelin et al. showed than collagen fibrillogenesis maybe stimulate by electric field under acidic conditions, while unaggregated collagen molecules were preferentially adsorbed to the electrified interface itself [62].

Collagen/CD thin films with different properties were prepared by layer-by-layer deposition technique. The different deposition conditions promote significant changes in the conformation of the polymer chains, which alters the interaction energy between the components and the morphology of the films. The conditions that decrease the surface charge, i.e., high pH and ionic strength, a greater agglomeration occurs in the polymer chains increasing the amount of deposited material. In particular, the increase in the ionic strength of the collagen solution promoted a controlled agglomeration process, known as fibrillogenesis, leading to the formation of a particular and interesting morphology of fibrils of different sizes. The preparation of self-assembled films with protein fibril deposition can be useful for the future development of new biomaterials since fibrils based functional materials are widely used in medical areas which require a subtle control of the materials properties. Finally, we expect to be able to build multifunctional nanocomposite films using this approach to a future preparation of films with well-defined properties and applications, including flexible implantable electronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the LMMA and associated projects FAPEMIG (CEX-112-10), SECTES/MG, RQ-MG (CEX-RED-00010-14), (APQ-02790-14) and APQ-02629-17. Capes and CNPq.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2019.03.060.
